ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chettinad Prawn Masala; 'shrimp dish' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a prawn-based curry dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chettinad Prawn Masala. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor; the masala/curry preparation context is not captured in the code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chettinad Prawn Masala or a sufficiently close curry dish equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chettinad Prawn Masala weighs approximately 300 g and is typically served as a main dish alongside steamed rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — prawn curry composite; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180, cooked shrimp) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides the closest published Indian prawn curry composition data. Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against ICMR-NIN prawn curry entries and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data. The composite is consistent with a Chettinad-style preparation using coconut milk and whole spices. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat fractions, potassium, and several micronutrients are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish type and are set to -1 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on prawn/shrimp curry with coconut milk; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chettinad Prawn Masala. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on the analogous category of seafood curry with coconut milk and whole spices from the University of Sydney GI Database and published international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, primarily from onion, tomato, and coconut milk; this composition strongly supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 6.0 × 3) / 100 = 4.5 g, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 316.67 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to 150–200 g; the potassium contribution from prawns, onion, and tomato partially offsets sodium load but does not eliminate the concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6.0 g per 100 g) and high protein (11.67 g per 100 g) from prawns produce a low estimated GI (~25) and a very low glycemic load of 4.5 per 300 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chettinad Prawn Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; pairing with cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice is preferred to maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 83.33 mg per 100 g (250 mg per 300 g serving) and saturated fat 5.0 g per 100 g from coconut milk are the primary cardiovascular concerns.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150 g and request light coconut milk substitution to reduce saturated fat intake; the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, peppercorn) provides partial benefit but does not offset the cholesterol and saturated fat load at full portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Strong anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger, piperine from peppercorn, fennel, cumin) with borderline saturated fat at 5.0 g per 100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chettinad Prawn Masala is cautiously suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its potent anti-inflammatory spice base; however, nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) should be monitored for individual joint flare responses, and coconut milk quantity should be reduced where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chettinad Prawn Masala is a fiery, aromatic South Indian seafood curry originating from the Chettinad region of Tamil Nadu, India, and widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish features succulent prawns simmered in a deeply spiced masala base built from whole spices — fennel seeds, black peppercorns, cumin seeds, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom — ground or tempered in oil, then combined with onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, dried red chillies, turmeric, and curry leaves. Coconut milk is added to create a rich, moderately thick gravy with a characteristic reddish-brown colour. The flavour profile is bold and complex: intensely peppery, mildly sweet from fennel and coconut, and deeply savoury with a lingering heat from both chilli and peppercorn. Texturally, the prawns remain firm and juicy within a silky, oil-kissed sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 150 kcal, 11.67 g protein, 9.33 g fat (5.0 g saturated from coconut milk), 6.0 g carbohydrate, 1.33 g dietary fibre, and 316.67 mg sodium. Cholesterol is 83.33 mg per 100 g. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 25 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when served without high-GI accompaniments. The spice blend — particularly turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), and black pepper (piperine) — confers evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and metabolic health. Sodium content warrants attention for hypertension management at full 300 g serving sizes. The dish is gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice, appam, or parotta.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chettinad Prawn Masala?
Is prawn masala good for diabetics?
What spices are in Chettinad masala?
Is prawn curry high in cholesterol?
Can I eat prawn masala if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of prawn curry?

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source. Values are consistent with ICMR-NIN prawn curry composition data and USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data, supporting retention at medium confidence. No exact published entry for Chettinad Prawn Masala exists in a primary approved source; the record is an adapted composite. GI is an analogous estimate. Several micronutrient fields are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish type and are set to -1 as fully documented in notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), B-vitamin panel (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), minerals (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for Chettinad Prawn Masala are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2023) or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish preparation as of 2026; these fields are set to -1 to indicate data unavailability rather than zero or null. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.07 g per 100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with minor trans-fat presence in coconut milk. All core macros (protein_g 11.67, fat_g 9.33, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a prawn curry with coconut milk. The ethnic_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the original input has been removed as it is incorrect for this dish. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been interpreted as the serving context — the dish's culinary origin is Tamil Nadu, India. GI is an analogous estimate (~25, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for seafood curry with coconut milk; no direct GI measurement for Chettinad Prawn Masala is published in approved sources.
